usageExample: She wanted to give up after the second failed attempt, but her coach encouraged her to try again.
tip: Use this when someone stops trying or quits something.

usageExample: I opened one article about coffee and went down the rabbit hole for two hours.
tip: Use this when someone gets deeply involved in a topic unexpectedly.

usageExample: Maya told a funny story to break the ice before the workshop started.
tip: Use this when someone helps people feel more comfortable socially.

usageExample: We ran out of milk, so I used oat milk in my coffee.
tip: Use this when there is none left of something.

usageExample: I am looking forward to seeing my cousins this weekend.
tip: This is followed by a noun or an -ing verb.

usageExample: After reading the instructions twice, he figured out how to install the app.
tip: Use this when someone understands or solves something.

usageExample: She turned down the job offer because the commute was too long.
tip: This can mean refuse an offer or reduce volume.

usageExample: The team came up with three ideas for the new campaign.
tip: Use this when someone creates or thinks of an idea.

usageExample: I need to catch up on emails after being away for a week.
tip: Use this when reaching the same level or getting updated.
alternatives: get updated or reach the same level
correctAnswer: get updated or reach the same level

usageExample: We usually hang out at the park after class.
tip: Use this for spending relaxed time with someone.

usageExample: He put off calling the dentist until the pain got worse.
tip: Use this when delaying something.

usageExample: Lena gets along with her new coworkers very well.
tip: Use this when people have a friendly relationship.

usageExample: Please do not bring up that old argument during dinner.
tip: Use this when mentioning a topic.

usageExample: They called off the picnic because of heavy rain.
tip: Use this when canceling an event or plan.

usageExample: Hold on for a second while I find your file.
tip: Use this when asking someone to wait.

usageExample: The manager promised to look into the customer complaint.
tip: Use this when investigating something.

usageExample: They argued yesterday, but they made up before lunch today.
tip: This can mean reconcile or invent a story.

usageExample: Only six people showed up for the early meeting.
tip: Use this when someone arrives or appears.

usageExample: When the director retired, her assistant took over the department.
tip: Use this when someone gains control or responsibility.

usageExample: Their plan worked out better than expected.
tip: This can mean succeed or exercise.

usageExample: Can you back up your claim with data?
tip: Use this when supporting an idea or making a copy of data.

usageExample: The small disagreement blew up into a huge argument.
tip: Use this when something becomes much bigger or explodes.

usageExample: Please carry on with your work while I take this call.
tip: Use this when continuing an activity.

usageExample: We checked in at the hotel just after noon.
tip: Use this for registering arrival or briefly contacting someone.

usageExample: You should check out the new bakery on Main Street.
tip: Use this when looking at, trying, or leaving a hotel.

usageExample: I am trying to cut back on sugar this month.
tip: Use this when reducing how much you use or do something.

usageExample: My neighbor dropped by to return the book.
tip: Use this for a short, informal visit.

usageExample: We got lost and ended up at a tiny restaurant near the river.
tip: Use this for the final result of a situation.

usageExample: The old chair fell apart when I moved it.
tip: Use this when something breaks into pieces or a plan fails badly.

usageExample: Please fill out this form before your appointment.
tip: Use this when completing a form with information.

usageExample: We found out that the museum is closed on Mondays.
tip: Use this when discovering information.

usageExample: He arrived late every day and somehow got away with it.
tip: Use this when someone avoids punishment for something wrong.

usageExample: It took her months to get over the disappointment.
tip: Use this when recovering from an illness, emotion, or difficulty.

usageExample: After an hour of negotiation, the company finally gave in.
tip: Use this when someone stops resisting or agrees reluctantly.

usageExample: Let us go over the plan one more time before the meeting.
tip: Use this when reviewing something carefully.

usageExample: Students must hand in their essays by Friday.
tip: Use this when submitting work or documents.

usageExample: It is hard to keep up with all the new software updates.
tip: Use this when staying at the same pace or level.
alternatives: stay current or at the same pace
correctAnswer: stay current or at the same pace

usageExample: You left out an important detail in your summary.
tip: Use this when omitting something.

usageExample: I felt I let down my team when I missed the deadline.
tip: Use this when disappointing someone.

usageExample: Could you look after my plants while I am traveling?
tip: Use this when caring for someone or something.

usageExample: Many young designers look up to her because of her creativity.
tip: Use this when admiring or respecting someone.

usageExample: Her grandfather passed away peacefully at home.
tip: This is a gentle way to say someone died.

usageExample: He passed out after standing in the heat for too long.
tip: Use this when someone faints.

usageExample: I will pick up the kids from school at three.
tip: This can mean collect, lift, or learn informally.

usageExample: She pointed out a typo on the first page.
tip: Use this when drawing attention to something.

usageExample: I cannot put up with the noise from the construction anymore.
tip: Use this when tolerating something unpleasant.

usageExample: I ran into an old friend at the grocery store.
tip: Use this when meeting someone unexpectedly.

usageExample: They set up the chairs before the guests arrived.
tip: Use this when arranging or preparing something.

usageExample: We need to sort out the payment issue before shipping the order.
tip: Use this when solving or organizing a problem.

usageExample: Jon takes after his mother; they both love telling stories.
tip: Use this when someone resembles a family member.

usageExample: The plane took off twenty minutes late.
tip: Use this when an aircraft leaves the ground or when removing clothing.

usageExample: Do not throw away that receipt until the package arrives.
tip: Use this when putting something in the trash.

usageExample: She tried on three jackets before choosing the blue one.
tip: Use this when putting on clothes to see if they fit.

usageExample: Can you turn up the music a little?
tip: This can mean increase volume or appear unexpectedly.

usageExample: I usually wake up before my alarm rings.
tip: Use this when someone stops sleeping.

usageExample: Those shoes wore out after years of walking to work.
tip: Use this when something becomes unusable from use or someone becomes exhausted.
alternatives: become exhausted or damaged by use
correctAnswer: become exhausted or damaged by use

usageExample: Losing that job was a blessing in disguise because it pushed him to start his own business.
tip: Use this for something that seems bad but leads to a good result.
alternatives: a bad thing that leads to good
correctAnswer: a bad thing that leads to good

usageExample: Stop beating around the bush and tell me what happened.
tip: Use this when someone avoids saying something directly.

usageExample: You finally sent the thank-you note; better late than never.
tip: Use this when something happens late but is still better than not happening.
alternatives: late is better than not at all
correctAnswer: late is better than not at all

usageExample: I need to bite the bullet and book the expensive repair.
tip: Use this when facing something difficult but necessary.

usageExample: He is trying to break the habit of checking his phone every five minutes.
tip: Use this when stopping a repeated behavior.

usageExample: The inspector does everything by the book.
tip: Use this when following rules exactly.

usageExample: We finished the main tasks, so let us call it a day.
tip: Use this when stopping work for the day.

expression: cost an arm and a leg
usageExample: That designer sofa costs an arm and a leg.
tip: Use this when something is very expensive.

usageExample: The builder cut corners, and now the roof leaks.
tip: Use this when someone does something cheaply or carelessly to save time or money.
alternatives: do something poorly to save resources
correctAnswer: do something poorly to save resources

usageExample: A little lemon juice should do the trick for this sauce.
tip: Use this when something solves the problem.

usageExample: I dropped the ball by forgetting to send the invoice.
tip: Use this when someone makes a mistake by failing to do their responsibility.

usageExample: Saving half your paycheck is easier said than done.
tip: Use this when something sounds simple but is difficult.
alternatives: harder to do than to say
correctAnswer: harder to do than to say

usageExample: Every now and then, I like to take a long walk without my phone.
tip: Use this for something that happens occasionally.

usageExample: After missing the deadline, he had to face the music.
tip: Use this when accepting consequences.

usageExample: She got cold feet before signing the contract.
tip: Use this when someone becomes nervous before doing something important.

usageExample: After a few lessons, I got the hang of driving a manual car.
tip: Use this when learning how to do something.

usageExample: Can you give me a hand with these boxes?
tip: Use this when asking for or offering help.

usageExample: Our support team goes the extra mile for customers.
tip: Use this when someone makes extra effort.

expression: hit the nail on the head
usageExample: Your comment hit the nail on the head; that is exactly the problem.
tip: Use this when someone is exactly right.

usageExample: Do not worry; we are all in the same boat before the exam.
tip: Use this when people share the same difficult situation.

usageExample: Please keep an eye on the soup while I answer the door.
tip: Use this when watching or monitoring something.

expression: kill two birds with one stone
usageExample: By biking to work, I exercise and commute at the same time, killing two birds with one stone.
tip: Use this when one action achieves two results.
alternatives: achieve two results with one action
correctAnswer: achieve two results with one action

usageExample: It took a month for the new assistant to learn the ropes.
tip: Use this when learning how a job or process works.

expression: let the cat out of the bag
usageExample: I accidentally let the cat out of the bag about the surprise party.
tip: Use this when revealing a secret accidentally.

usageExample: With rent rising, many families struggle to make ends meet.
tip: Use this when having enough money for basic expenses.

usageExample: Tickets sold out yesterday, so we missed the boat.
tip: Use this when missing an opportunity.

usageExample: Training for a marathon is tough, but no pain, no gain.
tip: Use this when effort or discomfort is needed for improvement.

usageExample: I am still on the fence about moving to another city.
tip: Use this when someone has not decided yet.

usageExample: We only eat at that fancy restaurant once in a blue moon.
tip: Use this for something that happens very rarely.

usageExample: She called me out of the blue after ten years.
tip: Use this when something happens unexpectedly.

usageExample: The test was a piece of cake for her.
tip: Use this when something is very easy.

usageExample: Take a deep breath and pull yourself together before the presentation.
tip: Use this when someone needs to regain control of emotions.

usageExample: The outdoor market opens every Saturday, rain or shine.
tip: Use this when something happens no matter the weather or conditions.

usageExample: His email sounded polite, but if you read between the lines, he was frustrated.
tip: Use this when understanding hidden meaning.

usageExample: As a rule of thumb, I save twenty percent of every paycheck.
tip: Use this for a practical general guideline.

usageExample: They do not always see eye to eye on budget decisions.
tip: Use this when people agree.

usageExample: Sit tight while I check whether the room is ready.
tip: Use this when asking someone to wait patiently.

usageExample: Come on, spill the beans. Who got the promotion?
tip: Use this when revealing secret or private information.

expression: take it with a grain of salt
usageExample: He exaggerates a lot, so take his story with a grain of salt.
tip: Use this when you should not fully believe something.

expression: the ball is in your court
usageExample: I sent the proposal, so now the ball is in your court.
tip: Use this when it is someone else's turn to decide or act.
alternatives: it is your turn to act
correctAnswer: it is your turn to act

usageExample: Working remotely twice a week gives me the best of both worlds.
tip: Use this when you enjoy the advantages of two different things.

usageExample: My sister supported me through thick and thin.
tip: Use this for loyalty during good and bad times.

usageExample: I am feeling under the weather, so I will stay home today.
tip: Use this when someone feels mildly sick.

usageExample: Our vacation plans are still up in the air.
tip: Use this when something is undecided or uncertain.

usageExample: She burned the midnight oil to finish the report on time.
tip: Use this when someone works late into the night.

usageExample: We only have ten minutes, so let us cut to the chase.
tip: Use this when asking someone to get to the main point.

usageExample: I can help on weekends, but I draw the line at answering calls at midnight.
tip: Use this when setting a limit.

usageExample: Feel free to ask questions during the training.
tip: Use this to give permission in a friendly way.

usageExample: After years abroad, he moved back to Canada for good.
tip: Use this when something is permanent.

usageExample: I woke up early to get a head start on the project.
tip: Use this when beginning earlier than others.

usageExample: Our schedule changed twice, so we decided to go with the flow.
tip: Use this when accepting events without resisting too much.

usageExample: This week is stressful, but hang in there.
tip: Use this to encourage someone to keep going.

usageExample: I agreed to sell the car, but now I am having second thoughts.
tip: Use this when someone starts doubting a decision.

usageExample: In a nutshell, the project is behind schedule but still possible.
tip: Use this before giving a short summary.

usageExample: Keep your chin up; you will find another opportunity soon.
tip: Use this to encourage someone to stay positive.

usageExample: She knows the poem by heart and can recite it anytime.
tip: Use this when someone has memorized something.

usageExample: The team left no stone unturned while searching for the missing file.
tip: Use this when trying every possible option.

usageExample: I lost track of time while practicing guitar.
tip: Use this when you stop noticing how much time has passed.

usageExample: To make a long story short, we missed the train but found a better route.
tip: Use this before shortening a long explanation.

usageExample: Horror movies are not my cup of tea.
tip: Use this when something is not your preference.

expression: off the top of my head
usageExample: Off the top of my head, I can name five clients in that region.
tip: Use this when answering from memory without checking.

usageExample: She solved four problems in a row and felt like she was on a roll.
tip: Use this when someone has a series of successes.

usageExample: Your outline needs work, but you are on the right track.
tip: Use this when someone is moving in the correct direction.

usageExample: The party got out of hand after midnight.
tip: Use this when a situation becomes uncontrolled.

usageExample: We do not have a dinner reservation, so we will play it by ear.
tip: Use this when deciding what to do as events happen.

usageExample: She pulled strings to get her nephew an interview.
tip: Use this when using influence to make something happen.

usageExample: Her presentation raised the bar for the whole team.
tip: Use this when setting a higher standard.

usageExample: He apologized privately so the manager could save face in front of the team.
tip: Use this when avoiding public embarrassment.

usageExample: The service at that hotel is second to none.
tip: Use this when something is the best.

usageExample: Speak of the devil, Daniel just walked in.
tip: Use this when someone appears just after being mentioned.

usageExample: I cannot have lunch today, but can I take a rain check?
tip: Use this when politely postponing an invitation.

usageExample: Rosa took the lead during the product launch.
tip: Use this when someone begins directing or leading.

usageExample: We need to think outside the box to solve this design problem.
tip: Use this when using creative or unconventional thinking.

usageExample: After three failed prototypes, the team refused to throw in the towel.
tip: Use this when quitting or admitting defeat.

usageExample: Time flies when we are working on something interesting.
tip: Use this when time seems to pass quickly.

expression: to be on the safe side
usageExample: Take an umbrella to be on the safe side.
tip: Use this when doing something to avoid risk.

usageExample: Let us touch base tomorrow after you review the numbers.
tip: Use this when making brief contact to update or discuss.

usageExample: The supervisor turned a blind eye to small dress code violations.
tip: Use this when intentionally ignoring something wrong.

usageExample: He said he would clean the garage when pigs fly.
tip: Use this for something that will probably never happen.

usageExample: The job is competitive, but applying is worth a shot.
tip: Use this when something is worth trying.

usageExample: It took me a while to wrap my head around the new tax rules.
tip: Use this when trying to understand something complex.

usageExample: This traffic is terrible. You can say that again.
tip: Use this to strongly agree with what someone said.

expression: your guess is as good as mine
usageExample: When will the update be released? Your guess is as good as mine.
tip: Use this when you do not know the answer either.

usageExample: The investigation zeroed in on a problem with the payment system.
tip: Use this when focusing closely on something.

usageExample: After weeks of excuses, he finally came clean about losing the keys.
tip: Use this when admitting the truth.

usageExample: I scheduled the first meeting to get the ball rolling.
tip: Use this when starting a process.

usageExample: We should hit the road before traffic gets worse.
tip: Use this when leaving or starting a trip.

usageExample: Please keep me posted about the schedule changes.
tip: Use this when asking for continued updates.

usageExample: We need to make room for two more chairs at the table.
tip: Use this when creating space for someone or something.

usageExample: All those late nights paid off when she passed the exam.
tip: Use this when effort produces a good result.

usageExample: She saw through his excuse immediately.
tip: Use this when recognizing the truth behind a lie or trick.

usageExample: When the manager was away, Carla stepped up and led the meeting.
tip: Use this when taking responsibility or improving effort.

usageExample: If you stick to the plan, you will finish on time.
tip: Use this when continuing to follow a plan, rule, or decision.

usageExample: At the end of the meeting, she summed up the main decisions.
tip: Use this when briefly stating the main points.
